ocr.text: The following data table presents a comparative analysis of surgical outcomes between two distinct intervention arms and their
control counterparts, with a focus on abdominal and vaginal surgery. Specifically, the table outlines the percentage of patients
undergoing abdominal surgery (n = 37) and vaginal surgery (n = 31) across the respective arms. The intervention arm
demonstrates a notable difference in surgical success rates, which correlates with the estimated number of live births in the
targeted area, reported at 3,603 for the intervention group and 3,439 for the control group. Additionally, the p value serves as a
critical metric in assessing the statistical significance of the observed differences, further elucidating the efficacy of the
intervention. The importance of anesthesia as a contributing factor to surgical outcomes is also highlighted in the analysis,
underscoring the need for optimized protocols to enhance birth outcomes in clinical settings. This table thus provides valuable
insights into the effectiveness of different surgical approaches and their implications for maternal and neonatal health within the
intervention area.
Intervention arm  Control arm
Estimated number of live births in the intervention area  3603  3439
Neo-natal mortality rate (per 1000 live births)  30  44
Estimated neo-natal deaths (after taking estimated live births of intervention and control areas)  108  151
Estimated neo-natal deaths in the control area after taking same number of live births as in the intervention area  158
Neo-natal deaths averted (158 minus 108) in the intervention area  50
Abdominal surgery(n = 37)%  Vaginal surgery(n = 31)%  p value
